topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a monotonous soundtrack to my rising anxiety. I stared at the application form, the ink blurring slightly under my thumb. Liam, my oldest friend since kindergarten, had been the one to tell me about this opening at Stellar Dynamics. Now, seeing his name beside mine, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I reread the bullet points, trying to find a flaw, a reason why I shouldn't be here. Every time I thought about the interview, my hands started to tremble, a slight, uncontrollable vibration that made holding the pen difficult. I felt a profound sense of wrongness settling over me, heavy and suffocating, as if I were carrying a secret burden that was about to be exposed.

[story 2]

I paced the length of my cramped apartment, the worn floorboards protesting under my hurried steps. The rejection letter from the other applicant had already landed in my inbox, a stark, unwelcome confirmation. I looked down at my own meticulously crafted resume, the paper suddenly feeling flimsy and inadequate. A sharp, acidic taste rose in my mouth. I kept replaying the conversation I’d had with Liam—his excitement, his confidence—and contrasted it with my own internal turmoil. When I finally sat down to edit my cover letter, my shoulders slumped forward, and I chewed nervously on the end of my pen. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming in my chest; it was a heavy, unwelcome weight pressing down on my chest cavity.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug had gone cold, mirroring the sudden chill that had taken root in my bones. I kept glancing at the clock, the seconds stretching into agonizing eternities. This opportunity felt monumental, yet simultaneously tainted. I remembered Liam mentioning how much he wanted this position, the sheer eagerness shining in his eyes. I tried to focus on the technical specifications of the role, but my mind kept drifting to the shared history we had, and the unfairness of the situation. A tightness constricted my throat, making it hard to draw a full breath. I picked up my phone, intending to call him, but the words caught in my throat, dissolving into a dry, rasping sound. I just stood there, paralyzed by the awareness of the choice I was about to make.
